---
title: "Salary & Compensation Trends"
author: "Nutchanon Chaiyaratana"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cerulean
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    embed-resources: true
date-modified: today
date-format: long
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  freeze: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---
## Overview

This section analyzes salary and compensation patterns in 2024 job postings using the Lightcast dataset. The goal is to understand how salaries vary across occupations, industries, locations, employment types, remote work arrangements, and AI-related skills.

Because salary information is not available for every job posting, this analysis focuses on records with valid salary values greater than zero when comparing salary outcomes.

## Step 1: Load Data and Create Relational Tables
This section analyzes salary and compensation patterns in 2024 job postings using the Lightcast dataset. The goal is to understand how salaries vary across occupations, industries, locations, employment types, and remote work arrangements.

Because salary information is not available for every job posting, this analysis focuses on records with valid salary values greater than zero.

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| output: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, monotonically_increasing_id, when

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("SalaryCompensationTrends") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

# Load cleaned dataset from data_cleaning.qmd
df_clean = spark.read.parquet("data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet")
df_clean.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs_clean")

print("Cleaned rows:", df_clean.count())
print("Cleaned columns:", len(df_clean.columns))
print("Has imputed salary column:", "ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED" in df_clean.columns)

# companies table
companies_df = df_clean.select(
    col("COMPANY").alias("company_id"),
    col("COMPANY").alias("company"),
    col("COMPANY_NAME").alias("company_name"),
    col("COMPANY_RAW").alias("company_raw"),
    col("COMPANY_IS_STAFFING").alias("company_is_staffing")
).dropDuplicates(["company_id"])

companies_df.createOrReplaceTempView("companies")

# industries table
industries_df = df_clean.select(
    col("NAICS_2022_6").alias("naics_2022_6"),
    col("NAICS_2022_6_NAME").alias("naics_2022_6_name")
).dropDuplicates()

industries_df = industries_df.withColumn(
    "industry_id",
    monotonically_increasing_id()
)

industries_df = industries_df.select(
    "industry_id",
    "naics_2022_6",
    "naics_2022_6_name"
)

industries_df.createOrReplaceTempView("industries")

# locations table
locations_df = df_clean.select(
    col("LOCATION").alias("location"),
    col("CITY_NAME").alias("city_name"),
    col("STATE_NAME").alias("state_name"),
    col("COUNTY_NAME").alias("county_name"),
    col("MSA").alias("msa"),
    col("MSA_NAME").alias("msa_name")
).dropDuplicates()

locations_df = locations_df.withColumn(
    "location_id",
    monotonically_increasing_id()
)

locations_df = locations_df.select(
    "location_id",
    "location",
    "city_name",
    "state_name",
    "county_name",
    "msa",
    "msa_name"
)

locations_df.createOrReplaceTempView("locations")

# occupations table
occupations_df = df_clean.select(
    col("SOC_5").alias("soc_5"),
    col("SOC_5_NAME").alias("soc_5_name"),
    col("LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION").alias("specialized_occupation"),
    col("LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME").alias("specialized_occupation_name"),
    col("LOT_OCCUPATION_GROUP").alias("occupation_group"),
    col("LOT_OCCUPATION_GROUP_NAME").alias("occupation_group_name")
).dropDuplicates()

occupations_df = occupations_df.withColumn(
    "occupation_id",
    monotonically_increasing_id()
)

occupations_df = occupations_df.select(
    "occupation_id",
    "soc_5",
    "soc_5_name",
    "specialized_occupation",
    "specialized_occupation_name",
    "occupation_group",
    "occupation_group_name"
)

occupations_df.createOrReplaceTempView("occupations")

# job postings table
job_with_industry = df_clean.join(
    industries_df,
    (
        (df_clean["NAICS_2022_6"] == industries_df["naics_2022_6"]) &
        (df_clean["NAICS_2022_6_NAME"] == industries_df["naics_2022_6_name"])
    ),
    "left"
)

job_with_location = job_with_industry.join(
    locations_df,
    (
        (job_with_industry["LOCATION"] == locations_df["location"]) &
        (job_with_industry["CITY_NAME"] == locations_df["city_name"]) &
        (job_with_industry["STATE_NAME"] == locations_df["state_name"]) &
        (job_with_industry["COUNTY_NAME"] == locations_df["county_name"]) &
        (job_with_industry["MSA"] == locations_df["msa"]) &
        (job_with_industry["MSA_NAME"] == locations_df["msa_name"])
    ),
    "left"
)

job_with_keys = job_with_location.join(
    occupations_df,
    (
        (job_with_location["SOC_5"] == occupations_df["soc_5"]) &
        (job_with_location["SOC_5_NAME"] == occupations_df["soc_5_name"]) &
        (job_with_location["LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION"] == occupations_df["specialized_occupation"]) &
        (job_with_location["LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME"] == occupations_df["specialized_occupation_name"]) &
        (job_with_location["LOT_OCCUPATION_GROUP"] == occupations_df["occupation_group"]) &
        (job_with_location["LOT_OCCUPATION_GROUP_NAME"] == occupations_df["occupation_group_name"])
    ),
    "left"
)

job_postings_df = job_with_keys.select(
    job_with_keys["ID"].alias("id"),
    job_with_keys["TITLE_CLEAN"].alias("title_clean"),
    job_with_keys["COMPANY"].alias("company_id"),
    col("industry_id"),
    col("occupation_id"),
    job_with_keys["EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME"].alias("employment_type_name"),
    job_with_keys["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"].alias("remote_type_name"),
    job_with_keys["BODY"].alias("body"),
    job_with_keys["MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"].alias("min_years_experience"),
    job_with_keys["MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"].alias("max_years_experience"),
    job_with_keys["SALARY"].alias("original_salary"),
    job_with_keys["SALARY_FROM"].alias("salary_from"),
    job_with_keys["SALARY_TO"].alias("salary_to"),
    job_with_keys["ANALYSIS_SALARY"].alias("observed_analysis_salary"),
    job_with_keys["ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED"].alias("salary"),
    job_with_keys["SALARY_IMPUTATION_STATUS"].alias("salary_imputation_status"),
    col("location_id"),
    job_with_keys["POSTED_DATE"].alias("posted_date"),
    job_with_keys["POSTED"].alias("posted"),
    job_with_keys["EXPIRED"].alias("expired"),
    job_with_keys["DURATION"].alias("duration")
).filter(
    col("id").isNotNull()
).dropDuplicates(["id"])

job_postings_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_postings")
```
## Skill Table

```{python}
from pyspark.sql.functions import split, explode, trim, lit, monotonically_increasing_id, regexp_replace, col

def create_skill_matches(column_name, skill_type):
    return df_clean.select(
        col("ID").alias("job_id"),
        explode(split(col(column_name), ",")).alias("skill_name")
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_name",
        regexp_replace(col("skill_name"), r'[\[\]"]', "")
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_name",
        trim(col("skill_name"))
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_type",
        lit(skill_type)
    ).filter(
        col("skill_name").isNotNull() & (col("skill_name") != "")
    )

specialized_skill_matches = create_skill_matches(
    "SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME",
    "specialized"
)

common_skill_matches = create_skill_matches(
    "COMMON_SKILLS_NAME",
    "common"
)

software_skill_matches = create_skill_matches(
    "SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME",
    "software"
)

certification_matches = create_skill_matches(
    "CERTIFICATIONS_NAME",
    "certification"
)

job_skill_matches_raw = specialized_skill_matches.unionByName(
    common_skill_matches
).unionByName(
    software_skill_matches
).unionByName(
    certification_matches
).dropDuplicates(["job_id", "skill_name", "skill_type"])

skills_df = job_skill_matches_raw.select(
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
).dropDuplicates()

skills_df = skills_df.withColumn(
    "skill_id",
    monotonically_increasing_id()
)

skills_df = skills_df.select(
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
)

skills_df.createOrReplaceTempView("skills")

job_skill_matches_df = job_skill_matches_raw.join(
    skills_df,
    on=["skill_name", "skill_type"],
    how="left"
).select(
    "job_id",
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
)

job_skill_matches_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_skill_matches")
```

### Validate Registered SQL Tables
Before running the salary analysis queries, I validate that all relational tables were created and registered in Spark SQL.

```{python}

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):

    styled = df.style

    if format_dict is not None:

        styled = styled.format(format_dict)

    styled = styled.set_properties(**{

        "text-align": "left",

        "white-space": "normal",

        "border": "1px solid black"

    }).set_table_styles([

        {

            "selector": "th",

            "props": [

                ("border", "1px solid black"),

                ("text-align", "left"),

                ("background-color", "#f2f2f2")

            ]

        },

        {

            "selector": "td",

            "props": [

                ("border", "1px solid black"),

                ("padding", "6px")

            ]

        },

        {

            "selector": "table",

            "props": [

                ("border-collapse", "collapse")

            ]

        }

    ])

    return styled

```
```{python}
table_counts = spark.sql("""
SELECT 'job_postings' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM job_postings
UNION ALL
SELECT 'companies' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM companies
UNION ALL
SELECT 'industries' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM industries
UNION ALL
SELECT 'locations' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM locations
UNION ALL
SELECT 'occupations' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM occupations
UNION ALL
SELECT 'skills' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM skills
UNION ALL
SELECT 'job_skill_matches' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM job_skill_matches
""")

table_counts_pd = table_counts.toPandas()

style_table(table_counts_pd, {
    "row_count": "{:,.0f}"
})
```

### Preview Companies Table

```{python}
companies_preview = companies_df.select(
    "company_id",
    "company",
    "company_name",
    "company_raw",
    "company_is_staffing"
).limit(5).toPandas()

style_table(companies_preview)
```

### Preview Industries Table

```{python}
industries_preview = industries_df.select(
    "industry_id",
    "naics_2022_6",
    "naics_2022_6_name"
).limit(5).toPandas()

style_table(industries_preview)
```

### Preview Locations Table

```{python}
locations_preview = locations_df.select(
    "location_id",
    "city_name",
    "state_name",
    "county_name",
    "msa",
    "msa_name"
).limit(5).toPandas()

style_table(locations_preview)
```

### Preview Occupations Table

```{python}
occupations_preview = occupations_df.select(
    "occupation_id",
    "soc_5",
    "soc_5_name",
    "specialized_occupation",
    "specialized_occupation_name",
    "occupation_group",
    "occupation_group_name"
).limit(5).toPandas()

style_table(occupations_preview)
```

### Preview Job Postings Table

```{python}
job_postings_preview = job_postings_df.select(
    "id",
    "title_clean",
    "company_id",
    "industry_id",
    "occupation_id",
    "location_id",
    "observed_analysis_salary",
    "salary",
    "salary_imputation_status",
    "posted_date"
).limit(5).toPandas()

style_table(job_postings_preview)
```

### Preview Skills Table

```{python}
skills_preview = skills_df.select(
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
).limit(10).toPandas()

style_table(skills_preview)
```

### Preview Job Skill Matches Table

```{python}
job_skill_matches_preview = job_skill_matches_df.select(
    "job_id",
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
).limit(10).toPandas()

style_table(job_skill_matches_preview)
```

## Salary & Compensation Trends
### Explore Career Categories and Sample Job Titles

Before classifying postings as AI-related or non-AI, I first review the career categories and sample job titles in the dataset.

```{python}
career_title_preview = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    o.specialized_occupation_name,
    COUNT(j.id) AS job_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.title_clean) AS unique_job_titles,
    CONCAT_WS(', ', SLICE(COLLECT_SET(j.title_clean), 1, 10)) AS sample_titles
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN occupations o
    ON j.occupation_id = o.occupation_id
WHERE
    o.specialized_occupation_name IS NOT NULL
    AND j.title_clean IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    o.specialized_occupation_name
ORDER BY
    job_count DESC
""")

career_title_preview_pd = career_title_preview.toPandas()

style_table(career_title_preview_pd, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_job_titles": "{:,.0f}"
})
```

## Explore AI-Related Skills

Before classifying job postings as AI-related or non-AI, I first explore the skill names available in the Lightcast skills table. Since the exact Lightcast skill names are unknown, I review the most common skills and then search broadly for possible AI-related skill terms.

### Top Skills in the Dataset

```{python}
top_skills = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    skill_name,
    skill_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT job_id) AS job_count
FROM job_skill_matches
GROUP BY
    skill_name,
    skill_type
ORDER BY
    job_count DESC
LIMIT 100
""")

top_skills_pd = top_skills.toPandas()

style_table(top_skills_pd, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}"
})
```

### Possible AI-Related Skills

```{python}
possible_ai_skills = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    skill_name,
    skill_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT job_id) AS job_count
FROM job_skill_matches
WHERE
    LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%machine%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%learning%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%artificial%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%deep%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%neural%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%natural language%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%nlp%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%vision%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%generative%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%language model%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%predictive%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%model%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%algorithm%'
    OR LOWER(skill_name) LIKE '%data science%'
GROUP BY
    skill_name,
    skill_type
ORDER BY
    job_count DESC
LIMIT 100
""")

possible_ai_skills_pd = possible_ai_skills.toPandas()

style_table(possible_ai_skills_pd, {
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}"
})
```

The table above is used as an exploratory step. It searches broadly for AI-related terms in Lightcast skill names. After reviewing these results, I use the confirmed AI-related skills to classify job postings into AI-related and non-AI groups.

## Analysis 1: Salary Differences Between AI and Non-AI Careers

This analysis classifies job postings as AI-related if they include AI or machine learning signals from either Lightcast skill tags, job titles, or job descriptions. Jobs without these AI-related signals are classified as non-AI. This combined method helps reduce missed AI-related postings that may not have AI skills tagged directly.

```{python}
ai_job_ids = spark.sql("""
SELECT DISTINCT
    j.id AS job_id
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN job_skill_matches s
    ON j.id = s.job_id
WHERE
    LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%generative artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%mlops%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%pytorch%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%artificial neural networks%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%machine learning model training%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%large language modeling%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%random forest algorithm%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%decision tree learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%ai engineer%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%ml engineer%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%large language model%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
""")

ai_job_ids.createOrReplaceTempView("ai_job_ids")
```

```{python}
ai_vs_non_ai_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)), 2) AS avg_salary,
    PERCENTILE_APPROX(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE), 0.5) AS median_salary,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.id) AS job_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.title_clean) AS unique_job_titles
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END
ORDER BY
    avg_salary DESC
""")

ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd = ai_vs_non_ai_salary.toPandas()

ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_share"] = (
    ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_count"] / ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_count"].sum()
)

style_table(ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd, {
    "avg_salary": "${:,.2f}",
    "median_salary": "${:,.2f}",
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_job_titles": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.1%}"
})
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ai-vs-non-ai-salary
#| fig-cap: "Average Salary: AI-Related vs. Non-AI Job Postings"
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

#| label: fig-ai-vs-non-ai-salary
#| fig-cap: "Average Salary: AI-Related vs. Non-AI Job Postings"
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false

import plotly.express as px

fig_ai_salary = px.bar(
    ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd,
    x="career_type",
    y="avg_salary",
    text="avg_salary",
    color="career_type",
    title="Average Salary: AI-Related vs. Non-AI Job Postings",
    labels={
        "career_type": "Career Type",
        "avg_salary": "Average Salary"
    },
    color_discrete_map={
        "AI-related": "#636EFA",
        "Non-AI": "#00CC96"
    }
)

fig_ai_salary.update_traces(
    texttemplate="$%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside",
    marker_line_width=0
)

fig_ai_salary.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=420,
    margin=dict(l=60, r=40, t=70, b=60),
    title=dict(x=0.02, xanchor="left"),
    showlegend=False,
    font=dict(size=12),
    yaxis=dict(
        title="Average Salary",
        gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.08)"
    ),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="Career Type"
    )
)

fig_ai_salary.show()
```

AI-related postings are identified using both Lightcast skill tags and AI-related keywords in job titles and descriptions. This combined classification reduces the chance of missing AI-related jobs that are not tagged with AI skills directly. The comparison uses only postings with valid salary values.

## How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?
```{python}

salary_distribution = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) AS salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
""")

salary_distribution_pd = salary_distribution.toPandas()

import plotly.express as px

fig = px.box(
    salary_distribution_pd,
    x="career_type",
    y="salary",
    color="career_type",
    points=False,
    title="Salary Distribution: AI vs Non-AI Careers"
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_title="Salary ($)",
    xaxis_title=""
)

fig.show()
```

## What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?
```{python}
jobs_with_ai = (
    job_postings_df.alias("j")
    .join(
        ai_job_ids.alias("a"),
        col("j.id") == col("a.job_id"),
        "left"
    )
    .withColumn(
        "career_type",
        when(col("a.job_id").isNotNull(), "AI-related")
        .otherwise("Non-AI")
    )
)
jobs_with_ai.createOrReplaceTempView("jobs_with_ai")

state_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    l.state_name,
    j.career_type,
    AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)) AS avg_salary
FROM jobs_with_ai j
JOIN locations l
    ON j.location_id = l.location_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
GROUP BY
    l.state_name,
    j.career_type
""")

state_salary_pd = state_salary.toPandas()

pivot_df = (
    state_salary_pd
    .pivot(
        index="state_name",
        columns="career_type",
        values="avg_salary"
    )
    .reset_index()
)

pivot_df["premium_dollars"] = (
    pivot_df["AI-related"] -
    pivot_df["Non-AI"]
)

pivot_df["premium_pct"] = (
    (pivot_df["AI-related"] -
     pivot_df["Non-AI"])
    / pivot_df["Non-AI"]
) * 100

# Add state abbreviation column for Plotly choropleth map
state_abbrev = {
    "Alabama": "AL", "Alaska": "AK", "Arizona": "AZ", "Arkansas": "AR",
    "California": "CA", "Colorado": "CO", "Connecticut": "CT", "Delaware": "DE",
    "Florida": "FL", "Georgia": "GA", "Hawaii": "HI", "Idaho": "ID",
    "Illinois": "IL", "Indiana": "IN", "Iowa": "IA", "Kansas": "KS",
    "Kentucky": "KY", "Louisiana": "LA", "Maine": "ME", "Maryland": "MD",
    "Massachusetts": "MA", "Michigan": "MI", "Minnesota": "MN", "Mississippi": "MS",
    "Missouri": "MO", "Montana": "MT", "Nebraska": "NE", "Nevada": "NV",
    "New Hampshire": "NH", "New Jersey": "NJ", "New Mexico": "NM", "New York": "NY",
    "North Carolina": "NC", "North Dakota": "ND", "Ohio": "OH", "Oklahoma": "OK",
    "Oregon": "OR", "Pennsylvania": "PA", "Rhode Island": "RI",
    "South Carolina": "SC", "South Dakota": "SD", "Tennessee": "TN", "Texas": "TX",
    "Utah": "UT", "Vermont": "VT", "Virginia": "VA", "Washington": "WA",
    "West Virginia": "WV", "Wisconsin": "WI", "Wyoming": "WY",
    "District of Columbia": "DC"
}

pivot_df["state_code"] = pivot_df["state_name"].map(state_abbrev)
pivot_df = pivot_df.dropna(subset=["state_code"])

fig = px.choropleth(
    pivot_df,
    locations="state_code",
    locationmode="USA-states",
    color="premium_pct",
    scope="usa",
    hover_name="state_name",
    hover_data={
        "AI-related": ":,.0f",
        "Non-AI": ":,.0f",
        "premium_dollars": ":,.0f",
        "premium_pct": ":.1f",
        "state_code": False
    },
    color_continuous_scale="RdYlGn",
    title="AI Salary Premium (%) by State"
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=550
)

fig.show()

```
## Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?
```{python}
remote_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    COALESCE(remote_type_name,'Unknown') AS remote_type,
    CAST(salary AS DOUBLE) AS salary
FROM job_postings
WHERE
    salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
    AND COALESCE(remote_type_name,'Unknown') != 'Unknown'
""")

remote_salary_pd = remote_salary.toPandas()

fig = px.box(
    remote_salary_pd,
    x="remote_type",
    y="salary",
    color="remote_type",
    title="Salary by Remote Work Arrangement"
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_title="Salary ($)",
    xaxis_title=""
)

fig.show()
```
## What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?
```{python}
industry_salary_monthly = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    i.naics_2022_6_name AS industry,
    MONTH(j.posted_date) AS month_num,
    DATE_FORMAT(j.posted_date, 'MMM') AS month,
    ROUND(
        AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)),0) AS avg_salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN industries i
    ON j.industry_id = i.industry_id
WHERE
    YEAR(j.posted_date) = 2024
    AND j.salary IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    i.naics_2022_6_name,
    MONTH(j.posted_date), DATE_FORMAT(j.posted_date, 'MMM')
ORDER BY industry, MONTH(j.posted_date)
""")

industry_salary_monthly_pd = industry_salary_monthly.toPandas()

top_industries = (
    industry_salary_monthly_pd
    .groupby("industry")["avg_salary"]
    .mean()
    .nlargest(12)
    .index
)

plot_df = industry_salary_monthly_pd[
    industry_salary_monthly_pd["industry"].isin(top_industries)
]

fig = px.line(
    plot_df,
    x="month",
    y="avg_salary",
    color="industry",
    markers=True,
    title="Monthly Salary Trends by Industry (2024)"
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    xaxis_title="Month",
    yaxis_title="Average Salary"
)

fig.show()
```
